Title: Merge two duplicate patient records selecting preferred record and verify surviving record
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator with Merge Patients privilege; two confirmed duplicate patient records exist in the system
Test_Steps: 1. Navigate to Data Management and open Merge Patients | 2. Enter the identifiers of both patient records | 3. Click Search to load both records side by side | 4. Select the record on the left as the preferred (surviving) record | 5. Review demographic differences highlighted between records | 6. Click Merge Patients | 7. Confirm the merge in the dialog
Test_Data: Patient A=John Tester (ID 10001); Patient B=Jon Tester (ID 10002); Preferred=10001
Expected_Result: The two records are merged into the preferred record 10001, the non-preferred record 10002 is voided and marked as merged, and the patient dashboard shows a single consolidated record

Title: Verify all encounters from non-preferred patient are transferred to preferred patient after merge
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; non-preferred patient has at least 3 encounters with observations
Test_Steps: 1. Open Merge Patients and load both duplicate records | 2. Note encounter count on each record | 3. Select preferred record | 4. Execute the merge | 5. Open the preferred patient dashboard | 6. Open the Visits and Encounters section | 7. Count and inspect transferred encounters
Expected_Result: All 5 encounters appear under the preferred patient 10001 with original encounter dates, providers and observations intact; no encounters are lost or duplicated

Title: Verify all identifiers from non-preferred patient are preserved on preferred patient after merge
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; both patients have distinct identifiers of different identifier types
Test_Steps: 1. Open Merge Patients and load both records | 2. Record identifiers on each patient | 3. Select preferred record | 4. Execute merge | 5. Open preferred patient demographics | 6. Review the Identifiers section
Test_Data: Preferred has OpenMRS ID 10001; Non-preferred has Old Medical Record 88-77-66
Expected_Result: After merge the preferred patient retains its own identifier and also carries the non-preferred identifier 88-77-66 as an additional identifier; no identifier is silently discarded

Title: Resolve conflicting demographic fields during merge using decision table of field sources
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; both patients have conflicting gender, birthdate and address values
Test_Steps: 1. Open Merge Patients and load conflicting records | 2. For each highlighted conflicting field choose the source record | 3. Select gender from record A, birthdate from record B, address from record A | 4. Review the resolution summary | 5. Execute merge | 6. Verify resulting field values
Test_Data: Gender: A=Female B=Male choose A; DOB: A=01-Jan-1980 B=02-Feb-1980 choose B; City: A=Boston B=Salem choose A
Expected_Result: The merged record reflects Female gender, 02-Feb-1980 birthdate, and Boston city exactly per the chosen sources; unchosen values are not retained in active fields

Title: Merge resolution decision table covering all combinations of preferred-vs-nonpreferred name selection
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; multiple name and address combinations exist on both records
Test_Steps: 1. Load both records into Merge Patients | 2. Apply combination row 1 (name=A, address=A) and merge a test pair | 3. Repeat for combination name=A address=B | 4. Repeat for name=B address=A | 5. Repeat for name=B address=B | 6. Verify each merged outcome against the table
Test_Data: Combos: AA, AB, BA, BB across 4 disposable duplicate pairs
Expected_Result: Each of the four field-source combinations produces a merged record whose name and address exactly match the selected sources defined in the decision table

Title: Prevent merging a patient record with itself
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator on the Merge Patients screen
Test_Steps: 1. Open Merge Patients | 2. Enter the same patient identifier in both the first and second search field | 3. Attempt to load both records | 4. Attempt to proceed to merge
Test_Data: Patient ID 10001 entered in both fields
Expected_Result: The system blocks the operation with a validation message indicating a patient cannot be merged with itself; no merge is performed

Title: Prevent merging when the non-preferred patient is already voided
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; one candidate patient record is already voided
Test_Steps: 1. Open Merge Patients | 2. Enter a valid active patient and a voided patient | 3. Attempt to load both records | 4. Attempt to execute merge
Expected_Result: The system prevents the merge and shows a clear message that a voided patient cannot participate in a merge; record state is unchanged

Title: Verify referential integrity of orders and allergies after patient merge
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator with database read access; non-preferred patient has active drug orders and allergy records
Test_Steps: 1. Capture pre-merge counts of orders and allergies for both patients via SQL | 2. Execute merge selecting preferred record | 3. Query orders table filtered by preferred patient id | 4. Query allergies for preferred patient | 5. Confirm no orphaned rows reference the voided patient as active
Test_Data: SELECT count(*) FROM orders WHERE patient_id IN (10001,10002); allergy rows for 10002
Expected_Result: All orders and allergies previously linked to the non-preferred patient now reference the preferred patient id; no active clinical row is orphaned or points to the voided patient

Title: Verify an audit log entry is created capturing who merged which patients and when
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; audit logging enabled
Test_Steps: 1. Note current timestamp and user | 2. Execute a patient merge | 3. Navigate to the audit log or person merge log | 4. Locate the merge entry | 5. Inspect captured fields
Expected_Result: An immutable audit entry records the acting user, both patient ids, the preferred record, and a timestamp; the entry cannot be edited from the UI

Title: Void (soft-delete) a patient record with a void reason and confirm it is excluded from search
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator with Delete Patients privilege; an active test patient exists
Test_Steps: 1. Open the patient dashboard for the test patient | 2. Choose the Delete (Void) Patient action | 3. Enter a void reason | 4. Confirm the void | 5. Return to patient search and search by name | 6. Search again including voided records
Expected_Result: The patient is soft-deleted with the void reason stored; the patient no longer appears in standard search but the underlying record is retained and retrievable by an admin including-voided search

Title: Require a non-empty void reason when voiding a patient
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator on a patient dashboard
Test_Steps: 1. Open the Delete (Void) Patient action | 2. Leave the void reason field empty | 3. Attempt to confirm the void
Expected_Result: The system rejects the action and displays a validation message that a void reason is required; the patient remains active

Title: Unvoid (restore) a previously voided patient and verify clinical data is restored
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; a voided patient with prior encounters exists
Test_Steps: 1. Locate the voided patient via include-voided search | 2. Open the record | 3. Choose Restore (Unvoid) Patient | 4. Confirm the restore | 5. Search for the patient in standard search | 6. Open encounters and observations
Expected_Result: The patient is restored to active status, reappears in standard search, and all previously associated encounters and observations are accessible again

Title: Verify void is reversible whereas purge permanently removes the record (soft vs hard delete)
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; access to both Void and Purge operations on disposable test patients
Test_Steps: 1. Void patient X and confirm it is recoverable via unvoid | 2. Unvoid patient X to confirm reversibility | 3. Void patient Y | 4. Purge (permanently delete) patient Y | 5. Attempt to locate patient Y via include-voided search
Test_Data: Patient X=10060 (void/unvoid); Patient Y=10061 (void then purge)
Expected_Result: Patient X transitions active to voided to active reversibly; Patient Y after purge is permanently removed and cannot be found by any search or restore action

Title: Validate full state transition lifecycle active to voided to unvoided to purged
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator with privileges for void, unvoid and purge
Test_Steps: 1. Create a disposable patient (state=active) | 2. Void it (state=voided) | 3. Unvoid it (state=active) | 4. Void it again (state=voided) | 5. Purge it (state=purged) | 6. After each transition verify visibility and recoverability
Expected_Result: Each transition behaves per the state model: voided is hidden but recoverable, unvoided is fully active, purged is irrecoverable; no invalid transition (e.g. unvoid after purge) is permitted

Title: Reject creation of a patient with a duplicate identifier of a unique identifier type
Preconditions: Logged in as Registration Clerk; an existing patient already holds identifier value of a uniqueness-enforced type
Test_Steps: 1. Start registering a new patient | 2. Enter valid demographics | 3. Enter the identifier value already used by an existing patient | 4. Attempt to save the record
Test_Data: Existing OpenMRS ID = 10001-5; new patient reuses 10001-5
Expected_Result: The system rejects the save with a duplicate identifier validation error; no new patient record is created

Title: Validate identifier check digit (Luhn mod-30) for OpenMRS identifier type
Preconditions: Logged in as Registration Clerk; OpenMRS Identifier type with Luhn check digit validator is configured
Test_Steps: 1. Begin patient registration | 2. Enter an identifier with an invalid check digit | 3. Attempt to save | 4. Correct to a valid check-digit identifier | 5. Save again
Expected_Result: The invalid check-digit identifier is rejected with a format validation error while the valid check-digit identifier is accepted and saved

Title: Verify duplicate identifiers are surfaced after merge when both patients shared the same identifier value
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; both duplicate patients hold the same identifier value of the same type
Test_Steps: 1. Load both duplicates into Merge Patients | 2. Note both carry identifier value 10001-5 | 3. Select preferred record | 4. Execute merge | 5. Review identifiers on merged record
Expected_Result: The merged record retains a single instance of the shared identifier without creating a duplicate identifier row; uniqueness constraint is upheld post-merge

Title: Bulk void a list of test patients and confirm count and per-record outcome
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; a list of 50 disposable test patients exists
Test_Steps: 1. Select 50 test patients for bulk void | 2. Provide a common void reason | 3. Submit the bulk operation | 4. Wait for completion summary | 5. Spot-check 5 records | 6. Verify total active count decreased by 50
Expected_Result: All 50 patients are voided, the operation summary reports 50 succeeded and 0 failed, and spot-checked records show the void reason; active patient count drops by exactly 50

Title: Verify partial-failure handling in a bulk void when some records are already voided
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; a bulk list mixes active and already-voided patients
Test_Steps: 1. Submit a bulk void of 20 patients where 5 are already voided | 2. Wait for completion | 3. Review the per-record result report
Expected_Result: The operation completes with 15 succeeded and 5 skipped/failed clearly itemized; the bulk job does not abort entirely and already-voided records are not double-processed

Title: Boundary test bulk operation at maximum supported batch size
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; configured maximum bulk batch size is known
Test_Steps: 1. Prepare a batch exactly at the configured maximum size | 2. Submit the bulk operation | 3. Confirm it processes | 4. Prepare a batch of maximum size plus one | 5. Submit and observe handling
Test_Data: Max batch = 500; tests at 500 and 501 records
Expected_Result: The batch at exactly the maximum size processes successfully; the batch exceeding the maximum is rejected or paged with a clear message and no partial corruption

Title: Prevent purge of a patient with dependent clinical records unless cascade is explicitly chosen
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; a patient has encounters, orders and observations
Test_Steps: 1. Attempt to purge a patient that has dependent clinical records | 2. Observe the system response | 3. If a cascade option is offered, decline it | 4. Re-attempt purge without cascade
Expected_Result: The system blocks the hard delete due to referential dependencies (or requires explicit cascade confirmation); no orphaned encounter or observation rows are left behind

Title: Database verification that voiding a patient cascades void flags to dependent person rows
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator with DB read access; patient with person_name and person_address rows
Test_Steps: 1. Capture voided flags on person, person_name, person_address before void | 2. Void the patient via UI | 3. Re-query the same rows | 4. Compare voided flags and void_reason fields
Expected_Result: Dependent person_name and person_address rows reflect consistent voided handling and the patient row carries voided=1 with a void_reason and voided_by populated

Title: Merge two patients via REST API and verify response and resulting state
Preconditions: Valid REST session/credentials for a user with merge privilege; two duplicate patient UUIDs known
Test_Steps: 1. Send POST to the person merge or patient merge REST endpoint with preferred and non-preferred UUIDs | 2. Inspect HTTP status and response body | 3. GET the preferred patient resource | 4. GET the non-preferred patient resource
Test_Data: preferred UUID and nonPreferred UUID payload
Expected_Result: The API returns a success status, the preferred patient now owns the merged data, and the non-preferred patient is voided; response reflects the merge outcome

Title: Reject merge API call when caller lacks merge privilege
Preconditions: Valid REST credentials for a low-privilege user without merge rights
Test_Steps: 1. Authenticate as a clerk-level user | 2. Send the merge POST request with valid UUIDs | 3. Inspect the response status and body
Test_Data: Clerk token; valid preferred and nonPreferred UUIDs
Expected_Result: The API responds with 403 Forbidden (or 401 if unauthenticated) and performs no merge; both patient records remain unchanged

Title: Reject merge API call with a malformed or non-existent patient UUID
Preconditions: Valid REST credentials with merge privilege
Test_Steps: 1. Send merge POST with a syntactically invalid UUID | 2. Inspect response | 3. Send merge POST with a well-formed but non-existent UUID | 4. Inspect response
Expected_Result: Malformed UUID yields a 400 Bad Request and non-existent UUID yields a 404 Not Found; no merge occurs and no server error is exposed

Title: Verify FHIR Patient.active flag reflects voided status after soft-delete
Preconditions: FHIR R4 endpoint enabled; a patient exists and is then voided
Test_Steps: 1. GET Patient by id via FHIR R4 and note active=true | 2. Void the patient in the system | 3. GET the same Patient resource again | 4. Inspect the active element
Expected_Result: Before void the resource shows active=true; after void the resource shows active=false (or the patient is excluded per server policy), consistent with the soft-delete state

Title: Verify FHIR Patient $merge / link maintains references after merge
Preconditions: FHIR R4 endpoint enabled; duplicate patients merged via the system
Test_Steps: 1. Merge two patients | 2. GET the non-preferred FHIR Patient | 3. Inspect Patient.link for replaced-by reference | 4. GET the preferred Patient and confirm it remains active
Test_Data: Non-preferred Patient id and preferred Patient id
Expected_Result: The non-preferred FHIR Patient carries a link of type replaced-by pointing to the preferred patient (or returns 410 Gone per policy); the preferred remains active=true

Title: Validate FHIR Patient.identifier system URI and value correctness for identifier types
Preconditions: FHIR R4 endpoint enabled; patient has a configured identifier type
Test_Steps: 1. GET Patient resource | 2. Inspect each identifier entry | 3. Verify system URI matches the configured identifier type URI | 4. Verify value matches the stored identifier
Expected_Result: Each FHIR identifier element has a correct and stable system URI and a value that exactly matches the stored identifier; no identifier is emitted without a system

Title: Verify HL7 v2 ADT^A40 patient merge message updates the surviving identifier correctly
Preconditions: HL7 v2 inbound interface configured to accept ADT^A40 merge messages
Test_Steps: 1. Send an ADT^A40 message with MRG segment containing the prior patient identifier | 2. Capture the ACK | 3. Query the patient record by the surviving identifier | 4. Query by the prior (merged) identifier
Expected_Result: An AA acknowledgement is returned, the surviving identifier 10001 is retained, and the prior identifier 10002 resolves to the surviving record per merge semantics

Title: Verify HL7 v2 ADT^A23 delete patient information message is handled or rejected per policy
Preconditions: HL7 v2 inbound interface configured; policy for delete messages defined
Test_Steps: 1. Send an ADT^A23 delete patient information message | 2. Capture the acknowledgement | 3. Verify the patient state in the system
Expected_Result: The interface returns an appropriate ACK (AA if supported, AR/AE if not) and the patient state matches the documented policy; an unsupported message never silently corrupts data

Title: Pairwise coverage of merge variables: identifier overlap, encounter presence, address conflict, gender conflict
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; ability to construct controlled duplicate pairs
Test_Steps: 1. Construct pair 1 (idOverlap=yes, encounters=both, addrConflict=yes, genderConflict=no) and merge | 2. Construct pair 2 (idOverlap=no, encounters=oneSide, addrConflict=no, genderConflict=yes) and merge | 3. Construct pair 3 (idOverlap=yes, encounters=none, addrConflict=no, genderConflict=yes) and merge | 4. Construct pair 4 (idOverlap=no, encounters=both, addrConflict=yes, genderConflict=no) and merge | 5. Verify each merged outcome
Expected_Result: Each pairwise combination merges without error and the surviving record is internally consistent for identifiers, encounters, address and gender per the chosen sources

Title: Exploratory session probing merge behavior with unusual data (long names, diacritics, many encounters)
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; a duplicate pair with edge-case data prepared
Test_Steps: 1. Prepare duplicates with very long names, Unicode diacritics, and 100+ encounters on one side | 2. Attempt the merge | 3. Observe UI rendering, performance and field resolution | 4. Verify post-merge record integrity and search
Test_Data: Names with accents; 100+ encounters; 4000-char address note
Expected_Result: The merge completes within acceptable time, Unicode and long values render and persist correctly without truncation corruption, and the surviving record passes a basic integrity review; any anomaly is logged as a finding

Title: Verify no orphaned visits remain after merging patients with overlapping visit dates
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator with DB access; both patients have visits including overlapping dates
Test_Steps: 1. Record visit ids for both patients pre-merge | 2. Execute merge | 3. Query visit table for the preferred patient | 4. Query visit table for the voided patient id | 5. Confirm visit-encounter linkage consistency
Test_Data: Patient 10001 and 10002 with overlapping visit dates
Expected_Result: All visits reference the preferred patient, no visit references the voided patient as active, and each encounter still links to a valid visit; no orphaned or dangling visit rows exist

Title: Verify concurrent merge attempts on the same patient pair are serialized safely
Preconditions: Two admin sessions open; same duplicate pair loaded in both
Test_Steps: 1. In session 1 begin and submit the merge | 2. Near-simultaneously in session 2 submit the same merge | 3. Observe both responses | 4. Inspect the final record state
Expected_Result: Exactly one merge succeeds; the second attempt is rejected or no-ops gracefully because the non-preferred is already voided; no double-processing or data corruption occurs

Title: Verify voided patient data is excluded from clinical reports and patient lists
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; a voided patient previously appeared in a patient list report
Test_Steps: 1. Confirm patient appears in a patient list before voiding | 2. Void the patient | 3. Re-run the same patient list report | 4. Re-run an active patients dashboard widget
Expected_Result: After voiding, the patient is excluded from standard clinical reports and active patient lists while remaining retrievable through explicit voided/admin views

Title: Verify a Registration Clerk cannot access merge or purge functions
Preconditions: Logged in as a Registration Clerk without admin privileges
Test_Steps: 1. Attempt to navigate to the Merge Patients screen | 2. Attempt to access the purge/permanent delete action | 3. Attempt the operations directly via known URLs
Expected_Result: The merge and purge functions are not visible and direct URL access is denied with an authorization error; the clerk cannot perform destructive data operations

Title: Verify void reason, voided_by user and date_voided are captured and immutable
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; a patient is voided
Test_Steps: 1. Void a patient with a specific reason | 2. Open the record details or query audit fields | 3. Verify voided_by, date_voided and void_reason are populated | 4. Attempt to alter these fields from the UI
Expected_Result: All three audit fields are correctly populated with the acting user, timestamp and reason; the fields are read-only and cannot be edited after the fact

Title: Boundary test identifier length limits at minimum and maximum allowed characters
Preconditions: Logged in as Registration Clerk; identifier type with defined format/length
Test_Steps: 1. Enter an identifier one character below the minimum length and attempt save | 2. Enter at exactly the minimum length | 3. Enter at exactly the maximum length | 4. Enter one character above the maximum
Expected_Result: Identifiers below minimum and above maximum are rejected with a format error; identifiers at exactly the minimum and maximum boundary are accepted

Title: Prevent unvoid of a patient whose preferred merge target makes restoration ambiguous
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; a patient was voided as part of a merge into another patient
Test_Steps: 1. Locate the merged (non-preferred) voided patient | 2. Attempt to unvoid it | 3. Observe system handling and any warning
Expected_Result: The system either prevents the unvoid with a message that the record was merged, or warns that restoration may create a duplicate; the merge audit trail is preserved either way

Title: Bulk export of patient data set maintains record count and field integrity
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; a defined cohort of patients exists for export
Test_Steps: 1. Note the source cohort count and key fields | 2. Run the bulk export | 3. Open the export output | 4. Compare exported row count and sampled field values to source
Expected_Result: The export row count equals the source cohort count and sampled identifier, name and birthdate fields match the source exactly; no records are dropped or duplicated

Title: Smoke test that the Merge Patients screen loads and accepts two valid records
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; two valid patients exist
Test_Steps: 1. Navigate to Data Management | 2. Open Merge Patients | 3. Enter two valid patient identifiers | 4. Click Search
Expected_Result: The Merge Patients screen loads without error and displays both patient records side by side ready for comparison

Title: Verify Merge Patients screen is keyboard navigable and screen-reader labelled
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; assistive technology (screen reader) available
Test_Steps: 1. Open Merge Patients using keyboard only | 2. Tab through search fields, record panels and field-resolution controls | 3. Verify focus order and visible focus indicator | 4. Verify each radio/control has an accessible label announced by the screen reader
Expected_Result: All interactive elements are reachable and operable by keyboard with a visible focus indicator, and field-resolution controls have descriptive accessible labels meeting WCAG 2.1 AA

Title: Verify merge behavior is consistent across OpenMRS and adapter-backed platforms via Resource Adapter Layer
Preconditions: Resource Adapter Layer configured against at least two backends (OpenMRS and omiiCARE)
Test_Steps: 1. Perform an equivalent merge through the adapter against OpenMRS | 2. Perform the equivalent merge through the adapter against omiiCARE | 3. Compare surviving record structure, identifier handling and void semantics
Test_Data: Equivalent duplicate pairs on each backend
Expected_Result: The adapter produces functionally equivalent merge outcomes on both backends (surviving record, transferred encounters, voided non-preferred) within documented adapter mapping rules

Title: Verify provider, user and relationship references are repointed or preserved after merge
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator with DB access; non-preferred patient has relationships and a linked provider record
Test_Steps: 1. Record relationship and provider links pre-merge | 2. Execute merge | 3. Query relationship table for both patient ids | 4. Verify provider linkage on the surviving record
Test_Data: Patient 10002 has a parent relationship and provider link
Expected_Result: Relationships are repointed to the preferred patient without duplication, provider linkage remains valid, and no relationship row references the voided patient as an active endpoint

Title: Verify purge of a patient with audit/retention hold is blocked per compliance policy
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; a patient is flagged under a legal/retention hold
Test_Steps: 1. Attempt to purge a patient under retention hold | 2. Observe the system response | 3. Verify the patient record is unchanged | 4. Review the compliance log entry
Expected_Result: The purge is blocked with a compliance message referencing the retention hold, the record remains intact, and the blocked attempt is logged for audit

Title: Verify case and whitespace normalization prevents near-duplicate identifiers
Preconditions: Logged in as Registration Clerk; identifier type with normalization configured
Test_Steps: 1. Create a patient with identifier value ABC-123 | 2. Attempt to create another patient with identifier value abc-123 (different case) | 3. Attempt with leading/trailing spaces around ABC-123
Expected_Result: The system treats case and surrounding whitespace consistently per its normalization rules, preventing creation of conflicting near-duplicate identifiers or clearly allowing them by documented policy

Title: Verify obs and diagnosis records retain original encounter linkage after merge
Preconditions: Logged in as System Administrator; non-preferred patient has observations and a coded diagnosis tied to specific encounters
Test_Steps: 1. Record the obs and diagnosis with their encounter ids pre-merge | 2. Execute merge | 3. Open the surviving patient encounters | 4. Verify each obs and diagnosis still links to its original encounter and value
Test_Data: Obs vitals and ICD-coded diagnosis on encounter 5001
Expected_Result: After merge each observation and diagnosis remains attached to its original encounter with unchanged values and codes under the surviving patient; no clinical datum is reassigned or lost
